text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE VISIT NOTE
Patient: Margaret Chen | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [on file] | Fellow/Resident: [on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 6-month post-treatment surveillance visit. Patient is a 52-year-old East Asian woman, never-smoker, with HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16 ISH confirmed) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition HPV-positive staging), who completed definitive concurrent Platinol-based chemoradiation with IMRT approximately 6 months ago. She presents today for scheduled follow-up.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen was originally diagnosed approximately 6 months prior to today's visit with HPV-positive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with a single left level IIA cervical lymph node metastasis (2.1 cm on CT). She completed a course of definitive IMRT to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary site and gross nodal disease, with concurrent cis 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks. She received two of three planned Platinol cycles; cycle 3 was held due to nephrotoxicity (creatinine peaked at 1.32 mg/dL, subsequently recovered). Total cumulative cis dose was approximately 340 mg/m2.

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oropharyngeal mucositis with confluent pseudomembranous changes and severe odynophagia requiring percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement at week 5 of treatment, as well as grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral anterior neck with moist desquamation. At her 4-week post-treatment visit she was beginning to resume oral liquids and soft foods while continuing PEG supplementation. A post-treatment PET-CT obtained at approximately 12 weeks after completing chemoradiation showed complete metabolic response at both the primary site and regional nodes, which was confirmed clinically on flexible nasopharyngoscopy.

Since that visit, she reports progressive functional recovery. Notably, her PEG tube was removed approximately 8 weeks ago after she demonstrated adequate and consistent oral intake. She is now tolerating a near-normal diet and reports eating most foods without significant difficulty. She continues to have occasional trouble with dry or hard foods (such as crusty bread, raw carrots, and crackers), which she attributes to a combination of dry mouth and mild throat stiffness. She has not lost additional weight since the PEG was removed and feels her weight has stabilized.

Ongoing complaints include dry mouth, which she rates 2-3/10 in severity and describes as most bothersome in the mornings and during long talking sessions (she has returned to part-time teaching). She uses artificial saliva (Biotene spray) and takes frequent sips of water throughout the day. She denies otalgia, odynophagia, new lumps in the neck, hoarseness, hemoptysis, dyspnea, unexplained weight loss beyond that attributed to treatment, or any other new or concerning symptoms. She reports no otalgia, no referred ear pain, and no new voice changes. Energy level is described as good, approximately 85-90% of her pre-treatment baseline. Sleep is adequate. Mood is generally positive; she is engaged and optimistic. She has resumed approximately 50% of her teaching duties and is working toward full-time return.

Review of systems is otherwise negative for fever, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss beyond what is noted above, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel or bladder habits.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Fatigue improving; no fever, no chills, no night sweats, weight stable (see above)
- HEENT: Xerostomia grade 1, persistent but improved from nadir; occasional dry throat; no epistaxis; no new otalgia; no visible oral lesions noted by patient
- Neck: No new neck masses palpated by patient; mild tightness of anterior neck soft tissues, consistent with radiation fibrosis
- Respiratory: No dyspnea, no cough, no hemoptysis
- GI: Normal diet re-established; mild difficulty with dry/hard textures only; no odynophagia; no dysphagia to liquids or soft solids; no nausea, no abdominal pain
- Neurological: Residual mild tingling in fingertips bilaterally (grade 1 peripheral neuropathy from cis, unchanged from prior); no new neurological symptoms
- Endocrine: Mild fatigue possibly consistent with hypothyroidism (see labs below); no cold intolerance, no constipation, no hair loss
- Psychiatric: No depression or anxiety endorsed; coping well

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, RR 14, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.7°C, Weight 58 kg (pre-treatment weight was 62 kg; represents 4 kg below pre-treatment baseline, stable since prior visit).
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (slight limitation of strenuous activity, otherwise fully ambulatory).

General: Well-appearing East Asian woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented, pleasant and cooperative.

Head and neck:
- Oral cavity: Moist oral mucosa. No mucosal ulceration, no leukoplakia, no exophytic lesion. Tongue mobile and symmetric. Mild reduction in saliva pool on examination consistent with radiation-induced xerostomia. No trismus; mouth opening estimated full.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in clinic: Nasopharynx and posterior choanae normal. Oropharynx visualized; left base of tongue mucosa appears smooth and healing without visible lesion, exophytic mass, or ulceration. Right base of tongue normal. Soft palate and posterior pharyngeal wall unremarkable. Supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis normal; no lesions identified. Bilateral piriform sinuses clear. No obvious post-obstructive pooling of secretions. Findings consistent with a complete clinical response.
- Neck: No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Grade 1 subcutaneous fibrosis of bilateral anterior neck appreciated on palpation, consistent with post-radiation change. Skin of neck: hyperpigmentation and mild residual skin thickening of the bilateral anterior neck, representing expected late radiation dermatitis without active moist desquamation or wound breakdown. No carotid bruit on auscultation.
- Thyroid: Non-palpable; no nodules noted.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. PEG tube site: well-healed without erythema or discharge.
Extremities: No edema. Mild symmetric fingertip paresthesias on sensory exam, consistent with prior grade 1 peripheral neuropathy.

---

LABORATORY AND ANCILLARY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 6.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 13.0 g/dL, Plt 248 x10^9/L — all within normal limits.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (improved from nadir of 1.32 mg/dL during treatment, now essentially back to baseline of 0.78 mg/dL). Glucose 88 mg/dL. Total protein 7.0 g/dL. Albumin 3.9 g/dL (normalized from post-treatment nadir; improved nutritional status with resumption of oral diet).
Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (repleted after treatment; now within normal limits, ongoing monitoring given prior hypomagnesemia).
Thyroid function: TSH 4.2 mIU/L (at upper end of normal range; notable upward trend from pre-treatment baseline — see impression below). Free T4 not yet resulted at time of this note; pending.
LFTs: Within normal limits.

---

IMAGING RESULTS
6-month post-treatment CT neck with contrast (obtained prior to today's visit — reviewed in clinic):
No evidence of recurrent primary oropharyngeal malignancy. The previously enlarged left level IIA cervical lymph node has decreased further and now measures 0.7 cm, within normal size limits. Post-radiation changes present including mucosal thickening of the oropharynx and soft tissue fibrosis of the bilateral cervical soft tissues. No new or suspicious lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No destructive bony lesion. Overall, findings consistent with durable response to treatment.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive), status post definitive concurrent cis-IMRT — COMPLETE RESPONSE, ONGOING SURVEILLANCE.
Ms. Chen continues to demonstrate an excellent response to treatment. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy today shows a completely healed left base of tongue mucosa without any evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. CT neck confirms continued regression of the treated nodal disease and no new concerning findings. She is now 6 months out from the end of chemoradiation and reaching an important functional and surveillance milestone. The prognosis for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC with T2N1 disease and complete metabolic response is very favorable, with expected 3-year disease-free survival exceeding 85-90% in this patient profile (nonsmoker, HPV-positive, favorable AJCC stage). She was counseled accordingly. No evidence of active malignancy today.
Plan: Continue surveillance per head and neck oncology protocol — clinical examination every 3 months for year 2 (next visit in approximately 3 months). Annual cross-sectional or metabolic imaging as clinically indicated (next PET-CT or CT planned at 12-month post-treatment timepoint if no interim concerns). Continue flexible nasopharyngoscopy at each surveillance visit.

2. Post-radiation xerostomia, grade 1.
Ongoing but subjectively improving compared to nadir. Patient is managing well with Biotene oral mist, frequent water intake, and avoidance of caffeine/alcohol. No signs of radiation caries on examination today. Referred to dental oncology for annual evaluation and fluoride tray recommendation. Patient instructed to maintain meticulous oral hygiene and use prescription fluoride gel (1.1% NaF) nightly given cumulative radiation exposure to salivary glands and dentition.

3. Post-treatment dysphagia — improving, near-resolved.
PEG tube removed 8 weeks ago, with near-normal swallowing function restored. Mild residual difficulty limited to dry/hard food textures only. Recommend continued graduated diet advancement and self-directed swallowing exercises as instructed by speech-language pathology. Formal reassessment by SLP can be considered if symptoms plateau or worsen. No aspiration symptoms endorsed.

4. Radiation-induced cervical fibrosis, grade 1 — early changes.
Palpable subcutaneous thickening of bilateral anterior neck noted, consistent with expected late radiation fibrosis. Patient was counseled on importance of cervical range-of-motion exercises and stretching to mitigate progressive fibrosis over time. Physical therapy referral placed for targeted neck mobility program.

5. Post-radiation hypothyroidism — monitoring, possible early subclinical hypothyroidism.
TSH mildly elevated at 4.2 mIU/L (upper normal range, but trending upward from pre-treatment baseline of 2.8 mIU/L). Radiation-induced hypothyroidism is a well-recognized late effect of bilateral neck irradiation. Free T4 pending. If free T4 is low normal or subnormal, or if TSH continues to rise on repeat testing, initiation of low-dose levothyroxine will be considered. Endocrinology referral placed. Repeat TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks. Patient was counseled on symptoms of hypothyroidism to watch for (fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation).

6. Grade 1 Platinol-associated peripheral neuropathy — persistent, stable.
Mild symmetric fingertip tingling, unchanged and non-progressive since end of treatment. No functional limitation. No intervention warranted at this time. Continue monitoring. Patient instructed to report any worsening symptoms.

7. Weight and nutritional recovery — progressing well.
Weight 58 kg, down 4 kg from pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg but stable over recent months and improved from post-treatment nadir of 55 kg. Albumin normalized. Patient tolerating near-normal diet. No further formal nutritional supplementation required at this time. Encouraged calorie-dense foods and protein-rich diet to support continued weight recovery. Goal to return to baseline weight if possible.

8. Return to work and quality of life.
Ms. Chen has resumed part-time work as a high school music teacher and reports excellent motivation and psychological resilience. She was commended for her progress and participation in her own survivorship. She has engaged with local HPV vaccination advocacy efforts and was encouraged to continue. A formal survivorship care plan will be finalized and provided at the 12-month visit.

Patient verbalized understanding of the above. She has no questions at this time. Follow-up in 3 months or sooner as needed.

[Electronically signed — Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
